agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A production logistics system enforces a strict 1:1 Shipment-to-Carrier relationship with supervisor-gated reassignment. The product team requests an auto-split feature that would automatically divide overweight shipments across multiple carriers without supervisor approval.","bounded_context":"Shipment Management / Carrier Assignment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A unit of freight to be transported, currently guaranteed to have exactly one assigned Carrier."},{"term":"Carrier","definition":"The transport provider assigned to fulfill a Shipment."},{"term":"Sub-shipment","definition":"A proposed new concept — a child Shipment created by splitting a parent Shipment that exceeds the weight threshold."},{"term":"Carrier Assignment","definition":"The act of binding exactly one Carrier to a Shipment. Currently locked once set; only a Supervisor can reassign."},{"term":"Supervisor","definition":"A privileged role authorized to override Carrier assignment locks."},{"term":"Auto-split","definition":"Proposed automatic division of a Shipment into multiple Sub-shipments when weight exceeds 500 kg, each assigned to a different Carrier without Supervisor involvement."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a system-level data integrity constraint. If a Shipment exists without exactly one Carrier, or with multiple Carriers, downstream dispatch, tracking, billing, and liability attribution all break. The system is in a corrupt state.","testable_assertion":"For every Shipment in status >= ASSIGNED, COUNT(assigned_carriers) == 1."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Once a Carrier is assigned to a Shipment, only a Supervisor can reassign.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an authorization policy. A VP could decide to relax it for certain scenarios (e.g., automated systems acting with equivalent authority). The underlying integrity constraint is BR-1, not the approval mechanism.","testable_assertion":"Any Carrier reassignment on a locked Shipment without a Supervisor (or equivalent authorized role) credential must be rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"At the time of dispatch, every Shipment must have exactly one Carrier.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Physical constraint — a shipment leaves on one truck with one responsible carrier. Liability, tracking, and chain-of-custody depend on this. Violating it corrupts operational state.","testable_assertion":"No Shipment may transition to DISPATCHED unless it has exactly one assigned Carrier."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A shipment weighs exactly 500 kg — does it split or not?","expected_behavior":"Undefined in the request. Boundary must be specified (> 500 vs >= 500)."},{"scenario":"A split produces a sub-shipment that itself exceeds 500 kg — recursive splitting?","expected_behavior":"Undefined. Could lead to infinite recursion or require a minimum split unit."},{"scenario":"Auto-split occurs after a Carrier is already assigned — does this break the assignment lock?","expected_behavior":"Contradicts BR-2. Splitting a shipment that already has a locked Carrier assignment effectively reassigns (removes) the Carrier without Supervisor approval."},{"scenario":"Parent shipment is already in-transit or dispatched when weight is recalculated above 500 kg","expected_behavior":"Undefined. Splitting a dispatched shipment is operationally nonsensical — goods are already on a truck."},{"scenario":"One sub-shipment fails delivery but others succeed — what is the parent shipment status?","expected_behavior":"Undefined. The current model has no concept of partial fulfillment."},{"scenario":"Billing and liability — who is responsible for the original shipment contract?","expected_behavior":"Undefined. Splitting across carriers fragments the single point of accountability that BR-1 was designed to guarantee."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Splitting shipments across carriers may reduce per-carrier cost but introduces coordination overhead, potential duplicate handling fees, and complex liability attribution when damage or loss occurs across multiple carriers.","operational":"Introduces a parent-child shipment hierarchy that does not exist today. Every downstream system — tracking, dispatch, billing, reporting, SLA monitoring — must understand this new relationship. This is not a feature addition; it is a domain model evolution.","user":"Customers currently track one shipment with one carrier. Splitting fragments this into multiple trackable units, complicating the customer experience unless a unified tracking abstraction is built on top.","risk":"HIGH. The feature as described directly contradicts two production invariants. Shipping it as specified would corrupt the assignment model. The real risk is that 'auto-split' sounds simple but requires rethinking the core Shipment aggregate boundary.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Operations / Dispatch","impact":"Must handle coordinated multi-carrier pickups for what was previously a single shipment. New failure modes (partial pickup, carrier no-show for one sub-shipment)."},{"group":"Finance / Billing","impact":"Must reconcile multiple carrier invoices against a single customer order. Liability attribution for damage becomes multi-party."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"Receive multiple tracking numbers or must be shielded by a new unified tracking layer. Delivery experience may degrade if sub-shipments arrive at different times."},{"group":"Supervisors","impact":"Their reassignment authority is bypassed by the auto-split mechanism, potentially undermining operational controls they rely on."},{"group":"Engineering","impact":"The Shipment aggregate must be redesigned. This is not additive — it changes the fundamental unit of work in the domain model."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The spec contains a direct contradiction with production invariants (1:1 Carrier assignment and Supervisor-gated reassignment). Until the product team clarifies whether they intend to evolve the domain model or work within it, acceptance criteria cannot be responsibly defined. Confidence is below 50 due to the contradiction.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction between auto-split and the existing 1:1 Carrier-to-Shipment invariant is clear and unambiguous","The Supervisor bypass is a policy conflict that must be explicitly resolved"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the product team intends sub-shipments to be first-class Shipments or a new entity type","The lifecycle of parent vs child shipments","Billing, liability, and tracking implications — none addressed in the request"],"assumptions":["The existing domain model (1:1 Carrier assignment, Supervisor-locked reassignment) is accurately described and enforced in production","The 500 kg threshold is a business policy, not a physical or regulatory constraint"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The auto-split feature directly violates BR-1 (one Shipment = one Carrier) and BR-2 (only Supervisors can reassign). The request asks for multiple carriers per shipment without supervisor involvement, which breaks both rules simultaneously. This cannot be implemented without either (a) evolving the domain model to introduce a new aggregate (e.g., ShipmentGroup or parent/child Shipments) where each child independently satisfies BR-1, or (b) relaxing BR-1 to allow multi-carrier shipments, which would be a fundamental domain model change affecting every downstream system.","affected_stakeholders":["Operations","Finance","Customers","Supervisors","Engineering"],"options":["Option A: Introduce a ShipmentGroup aggregate. The original shipment becomes a logical parent. Each sub-shipment is a first-class Shipment with exactly one Carrier, preserving BR-1. The split operation is authorized as a system-level action (new policy, not a Supervisor bypass).","Option B: Relax BR-1 to allow multi-carrier shipments. This is a fundamental domain model change requiring audit of all downstream systems.","Option C: Require Supervisor approval for auto-split (preserving BR-2) and model sub-shipments as new Shipments (preserving BR-1). The 'auto' part suggests the split, but a Supervisor confirms it.","Option D: Reject the feature as incompatible with the current domain model and ask the product team to reframe the business need."],"recommendation":"Option A. It preserves both invariants by introducing a new aggregate rather than breaking existing ones. Each sub-shipment is a real Shipment with one Carrier. The split is a domain event (ShipmentSplit) that creates children from a parent, with clear lifecycle rules. However, this requires significant design work and the product team must accept the operational complexity it introduces."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Auto-split assigns multiple carriers to what is logically one shipment without introducing a parent-child model.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1: A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier."},{"violation":"Auto-split bypasses Supervisor approval for what is effectively a carrier reassignment (removing the original carrier and assigning new ones to sub-shipments).","business_rule_broken":"BR-2: Only a Supervisor can reassign a Carrier on a locked Shipment."}]}
